title: Kabab Koobideh

overview.description: Persian grilled minced meat kebabs seasoned with onion, saffron and spices, traditionally served with Persian rice and tahdig.

method: Grate the onions as finely as possible.
Place the grated onion in a clean tea towel or muslin cloth.
Squeeze firmly until as much liquid as possible has been removed.
Keep the onion pulp and discard the excess liquid.
Place the lamb mince and beef mince in a large bowl.
Add the squeezed onion, salt, black pepper and turmeric if using.
Add the saffron water.
Mix thoroughly with your hands for at least 5 minutes until the mixture becomes smooth, sticky and cohesive.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Divide the mixture into 8 equal portions.
With wet hands, shape each portion into a long sausage approximately 2–3cm thick.
If using wide metal skewers, press the meat firmly around the skewers and create shallow ridges along the length with your fingers.
If not using skewers, shape the meat into long, slightly flattened logs.
Preheat a heavy frying pan, griddle pan or cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat.
Cook the koobideh in batches if necessary.
Cook for approximately 3–4 minutes on each side until browned and cooked through.
Brush with melted butter during the final minute of cooking.
Place the tomatoes on the grill or in a separate pan.
Cook until softened and lightly charred.
Serve the koobideh immediately with Persian rice and tahdig.

learning.did-you-know: Koobideh comes from the Persian word koobidan, meaning 'to pound' or 'to beat', referring to the traditional preparation of working the meat mixture until it becomes cohesive.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a cohesive minced-meat kebab mixture and how onion moisture, fat and thorough mixing affect the texture and stability of grilled kebabs.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic Iranian family meal that may recreate the kebab and Persian rice dish the Chapman family remembers, while being adaptable for cooking on an induction hob.
chapman-tips: Don't skip squeezing the onion; excess onion liquid is one of the main causes of koobideh falling apart.
The meat mixture should become noticeably sticky and cohesive during mixing.
Use mince with enough fat; very lean mince produces dry and fragile koobideh.
Chilling the mixture makes shaping easier.
If using skewers, wide flat metal skewers work best.
If using a frying pan, make the logs slightly flatter so they cook evenly.
Don't move the meat too much during the first minute of cooking.
A small amount of saffron adds aroma and colour without making the dish sweet.
Grilled tomatoes are an important part of the classic presentation.
If the mixture is very soft, chill it for longer rather than immediately adding breadcrumbs or flour.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well either raw or cooked. Freeze raw shaped portions on a tray before transferring to a container, or cool cooked koobideh completely before freezing.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with rice, in flatbread, or crumbled into a salad.
